title: HttpClient basics
instruction:
Java 11+ includes `java.net.http.HttpClient`. The three main classes:

- `HttpClient` ŌĆö the client (reuse across requests)
- `HttpRequest` ŌĆö the request to send
- `HttpResponse` ŌĆö the response received

Run this to see the structure ŌĆö even without a network call.
successMessage: HttpClient is the client, HttpRequest is the request. The builder pattern sets options clearly. Version.HTTP_2 uses HTTP/2 if the server supports it, falls back to HTTP/1.1.

title: Making a GET request
instruction:
`client.send(request, BodyHandlers.ofString())` makes the HTTP call:

```java
HttpResponse<String> response = client.send(request, HttpResponse.BodyHandlers.ofString());
response.statusCode();  // 200, 404, etc.
response.body();        // response body as String
```

This is a synchronous (blocking) call ŌĆö the thread waits for the response.
hint: Wrap in try-catch for IOException and InterruptedException
successMessage: Status 200 = success. The JSON body contains userId, id, title, body fields. response.body() is just a String ŌĆö parse it with a JSON library or simple string manipulation.

title: Simple JSON parsing
instruction:
Without an external library, parse simple JSON using String operations or `org.json`.

For production, you'd use Jackson or Gson. Here we'll extract fields manually from the JSON string:

```java
// Extract value between: "key":\s*"value" or "key":\s*number
```
hint: Use split or substring with indexOf to extract values from JSON string.
successMessage: Manual JSON parsing works for simple cases. In production, use Jackson: objectMapper.readValue(json, Post.class) ŌĆö it handles complex structures, nested objects, arrays, null values, and type safety.

title: POST request with JSON body
instruction:
Send data with a POST request:

```java
HttpRequest request = HttpRequest.newBuilder()
    .uri(URI.create(url))
    .header("Content-Type", "application/json")
    .POST(HttpRequest.BodyPublishers.ofString(jsonBody))
    .build();
```
successMessage: 201 = Created. The server responded with the created resource including an assigned id. POST with JSON body is the standard pattern for REST API calls.

title: Async HTTP with sendAsync
instruction:
`sendAsync()` returns a `CompletableFuture` ŌĆö doesn't block the thread:

```java
client.sendAsync(request, BodyHandlers.ofString())
    .thenApply(HttpResponse::body)
    .thenAccept(body -> process(body));
```

Make 3 requests concurrently ŌĆö see how they complete faster than sequentially.
successMessage: Three requests fired simultaneously ŌĆö faster than sequential. CompletableFuture.allOf waits for all to complete. This is how Java web services handle multiple downstream API calls.

title: Build a REST client
instruction:
Final challenge ŌĆö no starter code.

Build a `JsonPlaceholderClient` class:
- `getPost(int id)` ŌĆö GET /posts/{id}, returns the body String
- `createPost(String title, String body)` ŌĆö POST /posts, returns status code
- `deletePost(int id)` ŌĆö DELETE /posts/{id}, returns status code

Test all three methods and print results.

Base URL: `https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com`
hint: Store HttpClient as a field. For DELETE: .method("DELETE", HttpRequest.BodyPublishers.noBody())

codeChecks.message: Create a REST client class with an HttpClient field.
Implement getPost(int id) for GET requests.
Implement createPost with a POST request.
Implement deletePost with a DELETE request.

successMessage: A complete REST client class. Reusable, well-structured, handles all three HTTP methods. Add authentication headers? One line in the HttpRequest builder. Chapter 21 done!
